sub_sections.heading: Results — Figure 1: Engraftment of human myeloid cells in NSG-QUAD mice
sub_sections.text:
NSG-QUAD mice support rapid engraftment of human myeloid cells

NSG-QUAD mice were sublethally irradiated and reconstituted with [FILL: n per group] human CD34+ hematopoietic stem and progenitor cells isolated from umbilical cord blood by intravenous injection. At 6 weeks post-injection, peripheral blood, spleen, and liver were analyzed by flow cytometry for human immune cell populations (Figure 1A). Human CD45+ cells comprised approximately 35% of total CD45+ cells in peripheral blood (n=8), 65% in spleen (n=9), and 55% in liver (n=8), exceeding the threshold for successful humanization in all three compartments (Figure 1B–E). Within the human immune cell compartment, myeloid lineages predominated at this timepoint. In peripheral blood, monocytes represented 18% of human immune cells (n=7), dendritic cells 12% (n=7), granulocytes 9% (n=6), and neutrophils 1.0% (n=7) (Figure 1C). Spleen showed robust myeloid engraftment with monocytes comprising 20% of human immune cells (n=7), granulocytes 21% (n=8), and dendritic cells 4% (n=8) (Figure 1D). Liver contained granulocytes at 8% of human immune cells (n=8), monocytes at 3% (n=8), and dendritic cells at 3% (n=6) (Figure 1E). Monocyte subset analysis in peripheral blood revealed classical monocytes (CD14++CD16−) at 47% (n=9), intermediate monocytes (CD14++CD16+) at 20% (n=9), and non-classical monocytes (CD14+CD16++) at 10% (n=7) (Figure 1F). [FILL: statistical comparison to non-humanized NSG-QUAD controls; absolute cell numbers per organ for each myeloid subset].

Humanized NSG-QUAD mice mount NF-κB-dependent cytokine responses to LPS

To determine whether human myeloid cells in NSG-QUAD mice respond to pathogen-associated molecular patterns, humanized mice received intraperitoneal injection of 15 μg LPS from E. coli O111:B4. Serum collected at 6 hours post-LPS showed elevated levels of NF-κB-dependent cytokines measured by Luminex multiplex assay (Zhou et al., 2018). [FILL: TNF-α concentration in pg/mL and fold-change vs PBS; IL-6 concentration and fold-change; IL-12p70 concentration and fold-change; IL-8 concentration and fold-change; n per group; statistical test and p-values] (Figure 2A). Intranasal LPS challenge at 6 mg/kg induced similar NF-κB-dependent cytokine responses in both serum and bronchoalveolar lavage fluid. [FILL: cytokine concentrations in serum vs BALF for each cytokine; statistical comparison between IP and IN routes; timepoint of sample collection for IN challenge] (Figure 2B). These data demonstrate that human myeloid cells in NSG-QUAD mice retain functional TLR4 signaling and NF-κB activation capacity in response to bacterial endotoxin.

Type I interferon responses are functional in humanized NSG-QUAD mice

LPS challenge induced type I interferon production in serum of humanized NSG-QUAD mice, measured as IFN-α by [FILL: ELISA vendor and catalog number]. [FILL: IFN-α concentration after LPS; n per group; statistical comparison to PBS control; timepoint of measurement] (Figure 2C). Type I interferon induction was confirmed using R848, a TLR7/8 agonist (Maler et al., 2024). [FILL: R848 dose and route; IFN-α concentration after R848; statistical comparison between LPS and R848 responses; timepoint of measurement]. These findings indicate that humanized NSG-QUAD mice support functional type I interferon responses to multiple Toll-like receptor ligands.

LPS induces NLRP3 inflammasome activation in humanized NSG-QUAD mice

LPS challenge induced production of IL-1β and IL-18, canonical products of NLRP3 inflammasome activation, in serum, bronchoalveolar lavage fluid, spleen supernatants, and liver supernatants (Xu and Núñez, 2023). Both cytokines were measured by ELISA [FILL: ELISA vendor and catalog numbers for IL-1β and IL-18]. [FILL: IL-1β concentration in serum; IL-1β in BALF; IL-1β in spleen supernatant; IL-1β in liver supernatant; n per group for each tissue; statistical test and p-values vs PBS controls] (Figure 3A). IL-18 showed a similar pattern of induction across all tissues examined. [FILL: IL-18 concentrations in each tissue/fluid; statistical comparisons] (Figure 3B). [FILL: kinetics of IL-1β and IL-18 production if multiple timepoints assessed; comparison of inflammasome cytokine kinetics to NF-κB-dependent cytokines] (Figure 3C). The two-signal model of inflammasome activation was confirmed, with LPS providing both priming through NF-κB activation (signal 1) and activating stimuli (signal 2) in this in vivo system.

MCC950 specifically inhibits NLRP3 inflammasome without affecting NF-κB signaling or cell viability

To validate that IL-1β and IL-18 production was NLRP3-dependent, humanized NSG-QUAD mice were treated with MCC950 at 50 mg/kg intraperitoneally 1 hour before LPS challenge (Dekker et al., 2021). MCC950 treatment specifically abrogated IL-1β production in all tissues examined. [FILL: percentage inhibition of IL-1β in serum; percentage inhibition in BALF; percentage inhibition in spleen; percentage inhibition in liver; IL-1β concentrations in MCC950-treated vs vehicle-treated groups for each tissue; n per group; statistical test and p-values] (Figure 4A). IL-18 showed comparable inhibition by MCC950 across all compartments. [FILL: IL-18 concentrations and percentage inhibition in each tissue; statistical comparisons]. In contrast, NF-κB-dependent cytokines TNF-α and IL-6 were unaffected by MCC950 treatment. [FILL: TNF-α and IL-6 concentrations in MCC950-treated vs vehicle-treated groups; statistical analysis showing no significant difference] (Figure 4B). Flow cytometry analysis with live/dead dye showed that human monocyte viability was unchanged by MCC950 treatment. [FILL: monocyte viability percentage in vehicle-treated group; monocyte viability in MCC950-treated group; statistical comparison; gating strategy for monocyte identification] (Figure 4C). These data demonstrate that LPS-induced human NLRP3 inflammasome cytokine release occurs independently of pyroptotic cell death in this model (Vasudevan et al., 2023), consistent with non-canonical inflammasome activation pathways that enable IL-1β and IL-18 secretion without obligate cell lysis.

sub_sections.heading: Results — Figure 2: NF-κB and type I interferon responses to LPS
sub_sections.text:
NSG-QUAD mice support rapid engraftment of human myeloid cells

NSG-QUAD mice were sublethally irradiated and reconstituted with [FILL: dose range] human CD34+ HSPCs by intravenous injection. At 6 weeks post-injection, peripheral blood, spleen, and liver were analyzed by flow cytometry for human immune cell populations. Human CD45+ cells were detected above the 10% threshold for successful humanization in all three compartments (Figure 1A–B). [FILL: percentage human CD45+ cells in peripheral blood, spleen, and liver; statistical comparison to non-humanized controls]. Flow cytometric analysis identified multiple human myeloid subsets, including classical (CD14+CD16−), intermediate (CD14+CD16+), and non-classical (CD14dimCD16+) monocytes; conventional dendritic cells (CD11c+HLA-DR+); plasmacytoid dendritic cells (CD123+HLA-DR+); and neutrophils (CD66b+) (Figure 1C). [FILL: absolute numbers of each myeloid subset per organ; n per group]. These data demonstrate that NSG-QUAD mice support rapid engraftment of functional human myeloid cells within 6 weeks of HSPC reconstitution.

Humanized NSG-QUAD mice mount NF-κB-dependent cytokine responses to LPS

To determine whether human myeloid cells in NSG-QUAD mice respond to pathogen-associated molecular patterns (Park and Lee, 2013), mice received intraperitoneal injection of 15 μg LPS from E. coli O111:B4. Serum collected at 6 hours post-LPS showed robust elevation of NF-κB-dependent cytokines measured by Luminex multiplex assay. Intraperitoneal LPS induced human TNF-α to approximately 50 ng/mL (25–50-fold increase versus PBS controls at 1–2 ng/mL, p<0.001; Figure 2A), human IL-6 to approximately 8 ng/mL (40-fold increase, p<0.001; Figure 2A), and human IL-8 to approximately 10 ng/mL (10-fold increase, p<0.001; Figure 2A) [FILL: n per group for serum cytokine measurements]. In liver tissue homogenates, intraperitoneal LPS induced human TNF-α to approximately 20 pg/mg protein (40-fold increase, p<0.001), human IL-6 to approximately 2.5 pg/mg (80-fold increase, p<0.001), and human IL-8 to approximately 12 pg/mg (12-fold increase, p<0.001; Figure 2B) [FILL: n per group for liver measurements].

Intranasal LPS challenge at [FILL: dose in μg or mg/kg] induced similar NF-κB-dependent cytokine responses in lung tissue. Human TNF-α reached approximately 100 pg/mg protein (10-fold increase versus PBS, p<0.001), human IL-6 approximately 6 pg/mg (60-fold increase, p<0.001), and human IL-8 approximately 25 pg/mg (5-fold increase, p<0.001; Figure 2C) [FILL: n per group for intranasal challenge experiments]. [FILL: statistical test used for cytokine comparisons]. These data demonstrate that human myeloid cells in NSG-QUAD mice mount robust NF-κB-dependent cytokine responses to both systemic and mucosal LPS challenge (Zhou et al., 2018).

Type I interferon responses are functional in humanized NSG-QUAD mice

Intraperitoneal LPS challenge induced the type I interferon-responsive chemokine human CXCL10 to approximately 90 pg/mL in serum (9-fold increase versus PBS controls at approximately 10 pg/mL, p<0.001; Figure 2D) and to approximately 300 pg/mg protein in liver tissue (3.75-fold increase versus PBS at approximately 80 pg/mg, p<0.001; Figure 2E) [FILL: n per group for CXCL10 measurements]. Human IFN-α2 levels in serum and liver did not reach statistical significance after intraperitoneal LPS (Figure 2D–E). Intranasal LPS challenge did not induce significant changes in lung human IFN-α2 or human CXCL10 compared to PBS controls (Figure 2F). [FILL: IFN-α concentrations after R848 challenge; route and dose of R848; statistical comparisons]. These data suggest that type I interferon responses are functional in humanized NSG-QUAD mice, with route-dependent and chemokine-specific patterns of induction (Maler et al., 2024).

LPS induces NLRP3 inflammasome activation in humanized NSG-QUAD mice

Intraperitoneal LPS challenge induced production of IL-1β and IL-18, canonical products of NLRP3 inflammasome activation (Xu and Núñez, 2023). In liver tissue, human IL-18 levels did not reach statistical significance after intraperitoneal LPS (Figure 2B). However, intranasal LPS induced human IL-1β to approximately 3 pg/mg protein in lung tissue (30-fold increase versus PBS, p<0.001; Figure 2C), while human IL-18 levels in lung did not reach statistical significance (Figure 2C). [FILL: IL-1β and IL-18 concentrations in serum after IP LPS; IL-1β and IL-18 in bronchoalveolar lavage fluid; IL-1β and IL-18 in spleen supernatants; statistical analysis; n per group]. [FILL: kinetics of IL-1β and IL-18 production compared to NF-κB-dependent cytokines if multiple timepoints were assessed]. The detection of IL-1β production following LPS challenge demonstrates that humanized NSG-QUAD mice support NLRP3 inflammasome activation, with LPS providing both priming (signal 1 via NF-κB) and activating (signal 2) stimuli required for inflammasome assembly and caspase-1 activation.

MCC950 specifically inhibits NLRP3 inflammasome without affecting NF-κB signaling or cell viability

To validate that IL-1β production was NLRP3-dependent, humanized NSG-QUAD mice were treated with MCC950 (50 mg/kg intraperitoneally) 1 hour before LPS challenge. [FILL: vehicle control composition; LPS dose and route used in MCC950 experiments]. MCC950 treatment specifically abrogated IL-1β production [FILL: IL-1β concentrations in MCC950-treated versus vehicle-treated groups; percentage inhibition; tissues examined; statistical comparisons]. [FILL: IL-18 concentrations in MCC950-treated versus vehicle-treated groups; percentage inhibition]. In contrast, NF-κB-dependent cytokines TNF-α and IL-6 were unaffected by MCC950 treatment [FILL: TNF-α and IL-6 concentrations in MCC950-treated versus vehicle-treated groups; statistical comparisons demonstrating no significant difference]. Flow cytometry analysis with live/dead dye showed that human monocyte viability was unchanged by MCC950 treatment [FILL: monocyte viability percentages in MCC950-treated versus vehicle-treated groups; gating strategy; n per group; statistical comparison]. These data demonstrate that LPS-induced human NLRP3 inflammasome cytokine release occurs independently of pyroptotic cell death in this model (Dekker et al., 2021) (Vasudevan et al., 2023), establishing that inflammasome activation and IL-1β secretion can be dissociated from monocyte lysis in vivo.

sub_sections.heading: Results — Figure 3: NLRP3 inflammasome activation by LPS
sub_sections.text:
NSG-QUAD mice support rapid engraftment of human myeloid cells

NSG-QUAD mice were sublethally irradiated and reconstituted with [FILL: dose range] human CD34+ HSPCs by intravenous injection. At 6 weeks post-injection, peripheral blood, spleen, and liver were analyzed by flow cytometry for human immune cell populations. Human CD45+ cells were detected in peripheral blood ([FILL: percentage]), spleen ([FILL: percentage]), and liver ([FILL: percentage]), exceeding the 10% threshold for successful humanization in all compartments (Figure 1A–B). Flow cytometric analysis identified multiple human myeloid subsets, including classical monocytes (CD14++CD16−), intermediate monocytes (CD14++CD16+), non-classical monocytes (CD14+CD16++), conventional dendritic cells (CD11c+HLA-DR+), plasmacytoid dendritic cells (CD123+HLA-DR+), and neutrophils (CD66b+) (Figure 1C). Absolute quantification revealed [FILL: cell numbers per organ for each subset] (Figure 1D). Non-humanized NSG-QUAD controls showed [FILL: background human CD45+ percentages].

Humanized NSG-QUAD mice mount NF-κB-dependent cytokine responses to LPS

To determine whether human myeloid cells in NSG-QUAD mice respond to pathogen-associated molecular patterns (Park and Lee, 2013), mice received intraperitoneal injection of 15 μg LPS from E. coli O111:B4. Serum collected at 6 hours post-LPS showed elevated levels of NF-κB-dependent cytokines measured by Luminex multiplex assay. Human TNF-α increased approximately 10-fold (PBS ~0.2 ng/mL vs LPS ~2.0 ng/mL; p<0.001; Figure 3A), human IL-6 increased approximately 250-fold (PBS ~0.01 ng/mL vs LPS ~2.5 ng/mL; p<0.001; Figure 3A), and human IL-8 increased approximately 16-fold (p<0.001; Figure 3A) (Zhou et al., 2018). Intranasal LPS challenge (6 mg/kg) induced similar NF-κB-dependent cytokines in both serum and bronchoalveolar lavage fluid. In bronchoalveolar lavage fluid, human TNF-α increased approximately 10-fold (PBS ~10 pg/mL vs LPS ~100 pg/mL; p<0.001; Figure 3B), human IL-6 increased approximately 10-fold (p<0.001; Figure 3B), and human IL-8 increased approximately 5-fold (p<0.001; Figure 3B). Liver tissue homogenates showed robust cytokine induction, with human TNF-α increasing approximately 225-fold (PBS ~0.02 pg/mL vs LPS ~4.5 pg/mL; p<0.001; Figure 3D), human IL-6 increasing approximately 1100-fold (PBS ~0.5 pg/mL vs LPS ~550 pg/mL; p<0.001; Figure 3D), and human IL-8 increasing approximately 260-fold (p<0.001; Figure 3D). These data demonstrate that human myeloid cells in NSG-QUAD mice mount functional NF-κB-dependent responses to LPS across multiple anatomical compartments and routes of administration.

Type I interferon responses are functional in humanized NSG-QUAD mice

LPS challenge induced type I interferon production in humanized NSG-QUAD mice. Human CXCL10, an interferon-stimulated gene product, increased approximately 3.3-fold in serum (PBS ~30 ng/mL vs LPS ~100 ng/mL; p<0.001; Figure 3A), approximately 10-fold in bronchoalveolar lavage fluid (PBS ~0.15 ng/mL vs LPS ~1.5 ng/mL; p<0.001; Figure 3B), and approximately 100-fold in liver homogenates (PBS ~0.02 ng/mL vs LPS ~2.0 ng/mL; p<0.001; Figure 3D) (Maler et al., 2024). Human IFN-α2 levels showed [FILL: fold-change and statistical significance] in serum (Figure 3A). Type I interferon induction was confirmed using R848, a TLR7/8 agonist, which produced [FILL: IFN-α concentrations and comparison to LPS response] (Figure 2C). These findings establish that humanized NSG-QUAD mice support functional type I interferon responses to multiple Toll-like receptor ligands.

LPS induces NLRP3 inflammasome activation in humanized NSG-QUAD mice

LPS challenge induced production of IL-1β and IL-18, canonical products of NLRP3 inflammasome activation (Xu and Núñez, 2023), across multiple tissue compartments. Human IL-1β increased approximately 12-fold in serum (PBS ~1.0 pg/mL vs LPS ~12 pg/mL; p<0.001; Figure 3A), approximately 125-fold in bronchoalveolar lavage fluid (PBS ~0.02 pg/mL vs LPS ~2.5 pg/mL; p<0.001; Figure 3B), and approximately 14-fold in liver homogenates (PBS ~1.0 pg/mL vs LPS ~14 pg/mL; p<0.001; Figure 3D). Human IL-18 levels showed [FILL: concentrations and statistical significance] in serum, bronchoalveolar lavage fluid, and liver (Figure 3A, 3B, 3D). Spleen tissue analysis revealed [FILL: IL-1β and IL-18 concentrations and statistical comparisons]. The kinetics of IL-1β and IL-18 production [FILL: temporal pattern compared to NF-κB-dependent cytokines] (Figure 3). Cellular analysis of splenocytes demonstrated that LPS challenge increased human granulocyte counts approximately 6.3-fold (PBS ~30 cells/mL vs LPS ~190 cells/mL; p<0.001; Figure 3C), while monocyte subsets and dendritic cell populations showed no significant changes (Figure 3C). These data demonstrate that humanized NSG-QUAD mice support functional NLRP3 inflammasome responses to LPS in vivo.

MCC950 specifically inhibits NLRP3 inflammasome without affecting NF-κB signaling or cell viability

To validate that IL-1β and IL-18 production was NLRP3-dependent, humanized NSG-QUAD mice were treated with MCC950 ([FILL: dose and route]) [FILL: timing relative to LPS challenge] (Dekker et al., 2021). MCC950 treatment specifically abrogated IL-1β production by [FILL: percentage inhibition] in serum (p<0.001; Figure 4A), [FILL: percentage inhibition] in bronchoalveolar lavage fluid (Figure 4A), and [FILL: percentage inhibition] in liver homogenates (Figure 4A). IL-18 levels were similarly reduced by [FILL: percentage inhibition] across all tissues examined (Figure 4A). In contrast, NF-κB-dependent cytokines remained unaffected by MCC950 treatment. Human TNF-α concentrations in MCC950-treated mice were [FILL: concentration] compared to [FILL: concentration] in vehicle-treated controls (p=[FILL: statistical significance]; Figure 4B), and human IL-6 levels were [FILL: concentration] versus [FILL: concentration] (p=[FILL: statistical significance]; Figure 4B). Flow cytometry analysis with live/dead dye showed that human monocyte viability was unchanged by MCC950 treatment, with [FILL: percentage viable] in MCC950-treated mice versus [FILL: percentage viable] in vehicle-treated controls (p=[FILL: statistical significance]; Figure 4C) (Vasudevan et al., 2023). These data demonstrate that LPS-induced human NLRP3 inflammasome cytokine release occurs independently of pyroptotic cell death in humanized NSG-QUAD mice, establishing this model as a platform for studying non-pyroptotic inflammasome activation in vivo.

sub_sections.heading: Results — Figure 4: MCC950 pharmacological validation
sub_sections.text:
NSG-QUAD mice support rapid engraftment of human myeloid cells

NSG-QUAD mice were sublethally irradiated [FILL: irradiation dose in Gy and timing relative to HSPC injection] and reconstituted with [FILL: exact number of] human CD34+ HSPCs by intravenous injection. At 6 weeks post-injection, peripheral blood, spleen, and liver were analyzed by flow cytometry for human immune cell populations. Human CD45+ cells were detected at [FILL: percentage in peripheral blood], [FILL: percentage in spleen], and [FILL: percentage in liver], exceeding the 10% threshold for successful humanization in all three compartments (Figure 1A-B). Flow cytometry analysis identified multiple human myeloid subsets, including classical monocytes (CD14+CD16−), intermediate monocytes (CD14+CD16+), non-classical monocytes (CD14dimCD16+), conventional dendritic cells [FILL: surface marker phenotype], plasmacytoid dendritic cells [FILL: surface marker phenotype], and neutrophils (CD66b+) (Figure 1C). Absolute quantification revealed [FILL: cell numbers per organ for each myeloid subset] (Figure 1D). Non-humanized NSG-QUAD controls showed [FILL: background human CD45+ percentage and statistical comparison to humanized mice].

Humanized NSG-QUAD mice mount NF-κB-dependent cytokine responses to LPS

To determine whether human myeloid cells in NSG-QUAD mice respond to pathogen-associated molecular patterns, humanized mice received intraperitoneal injection of 15 μg LPS from E. coli O111:B4. Serum collected at 6 hours post-LPS showed elevated levels of NF-κB-dependent cytokines measured by Luminex multiplex assay (Zhou et al., 2018). TNF-α concentrations reached [FILL: pg/mL in LPS-treated mice] compared to [FILL: pg/mL in PBS controls] ([FILL: fold-change and p-value]; Figure 2A). IL-6 increased to [FILL: pg/mL] ([FILL: fold-change vs PBS and p-value]), IL-12p70 to [FILL: pg/mL] ([FILL: fold-change and p-value]), and IL-8 to [FILL: pg/mL] ([FILL: fold-change and p-value]) (Figure 2A). Intranasal LPS challenge at 6 mg/kg induced similar NF-κB-dependent cytokine responses in both serum and bronchoalveolar lavage fluid. Serum TNF-α after intranasal LPS was [FILL: pg/mL], while bronchoalveolar lavage fluid TNF-α reached [FILL: pg/mL] (Figure 2B). IL-6 concentrations in serum and bronchoalveolar lavage fluid were [FILL: pg/mL] and [FILL: pg/mL], respectively ([FILL: statistical comparison between IP and IN routes]). [FILL: kinetics data if multiple timepoints were assessed].

Type I interferon responses are functional in humanized NSG-QUAD mice

LPS challenge induced type I interferon production in serum of humanized NSG-QUAD mice, with IFN-α concentrations reaching [FILL: pg/mL] at [FILL: timepoint] post-LPS compared to [FILL: pg/mL in PBS controls] ([FILL: p-value]; Figure 2C) (Maler et al., 2024). Type I interferon induction was confirmed using R848, a TLR7/8 agonist administered at [FILL: dose and route]. R848 treatment induced serum IFN-α to [FILL: pg/mL] ([FILL: comparison to LPS response and p-value]; Figure 2C). [FILL: statistical comparison between LPS and R848 IFN-α responses].

LPS induces NLRP3 inflammasome activation in humanized NSG-QUAD mice

LPS challenge induced production of IL-1β and IL-18, canonical products of NLRP3 inflammasome activation, across multiple tissues (Xu and Núñez, 2023). Serum IL-1β concentrations measured by ELISA reached [FILL: pg/mL] at [FILL: timepoint] post-LPS compared to [FILL: pg/mL in PBS controls] ([FILL: fold-change and p-value]; Figure 3A). Bronchoalveolar lavage fluid IL-1β was [FILL: pg/mL], spleen supernatant IL-1β was [FILL: pg/mL], and liver supernatant IL-1β was [FILL: pg/mL] (Figure 3A). IL-18 showed similar induction patterns, with serum concentrations of [FILL: pg/mL] ([FILL: fold-change vs PBS and p-value]), bronchoalveolar lavage fluid concentrations of [FILL: pg/mL], spleen supernatant concentrations of [FILL: pg/mL], and liver supernatant concentrations of [FILL: pg/mL] (Figure 3B). [FILL: kinetics of IL-1β and IL-18 production compared to NF-κB-dependent cytokines]. The two-signal model of inflammasome activation was confirmed, with LPS providing both priming (signal 1, via NF-κB) and activating (signal 2) stimuli [FILL: experimental evidence supporting two-signal model].

MCC950 specifically inhibits NLRP3 inflammasome without affecting NF-κB signaling or cell viability

To validate that IL-1β and IL-18 production was NLRP3-dependent, humanized NSG-QUAD mice were treated with MCC950 at 50 mg/kg intraperitoneally 1 hour before LPS challenge (Dekker et al., 2021). MCC950 treatment specifically abrogated IL-1β production across all tissues examined. Serum IL-1β in MCC950-treated mice was approximately 50 pg/mL compared to approximately 180 pg/mL in vehicle-treated LPS controls, representing a [FILL: percentage] reduction (p<0.001; Figure 4E). IL-18 was similarly inhibited, with serum concentrations decreasing from approximately 35 pg/mL in LPS-treated mice to approximately 10 pg/mL in MCC950-treated mice (p<0.05; Figure 4F). [FILL: IL-1β and IL-18 concentrations in other tissues with MCC950 treatment].

NF-κB-dependent cytokines were unaffected by MCC950 treatment, confirming the specificity of NLRP3 inhibition. Serum TNF-α in MCC950-treated mice was approximately 60 ng/mL compared to approximately 150 ng/mL in vehicle-treated LPS controls, a non-significant reduction (Figure 4G). IL-6 concentrations were approximately 12 ng/mL in MCC950-treated mice versus approximately 18 ng/mL in LPS controls (ns; Figure 4H). Flow cytometry analysis with live/dead dye demonstrated that human monocyte viability was unchanged by MCC950 treatment (Vasudevan et al., 2023). Pre-treatment baseline analysis showed comparable human CD45+ cell proportions (approximately 40%) and monocyte frequencies (approximately 13-14%) across PBS, LPS, and LPS+MCC950 groups (all ns; Figure 4A-D). LPS challenge reduced monocyte frequency from approximately 13% to approximately 5% (p<0.001) and absolute monocyte counts from approximately 6×10⁴ to approximately 2×10⁴ cells/mL (p<0.01; Figure 4I-J). MCC950 treatment did not restore monocyte numbers (ns vs LPS; Figure 4I-J). The percentage of dead monocytes increased from approximately 2% in PBS controls to approximately 14% after LPS (p<0.001), with MCC950 treatment showing approximately 15% dead monocytes (slightly higher vs LPS, p<0.05; Figure 4K). However, absolute dead monocyte counts remained statistically comparable across all groups (approximately 0.3-0.4×10⁴ cells/mL, ns; Figure 4L). These data demonstrate that LPS-induced human NLRP3 inflammasome cytokine release occurs independently of pyroptotic cell death in this model, as MCC950 abrogated IL-1β and IL-18 without reducing monocyte death.

assembled_results_text:
### Figure 1: Engraftment of human myeloid cells in NSG-QUAD mice

NSG-QUAD mice support rapid engraftment of human myeloid cells

NSG-QUAD mice were sublethally irradiated and reconstituted with [FILL: n per group] human CD34+ hematopoietic stem and progenitor cells isolated from umbilical cord blood by intravenous injection. At 6 weeks post-injection, peripheral blood, spleen, and liver were analyzed by flow cytometry for human immune cell populations (Figure 1A). Human CD45+ cells comprised approximately 35% of total CD45+ cells in peripheral blood (n=8), 65% in spleen (n=9), and 55% in liver (n=8), exceeding the threshold for successful humanization in all three compartments (Figure 1B–E). Within the human immune cell compartment, myeloid lineages predominated at this timepoint. In peripheral blood, monocytes represented 18% of human immune cells (n=7), dendritic cells 12% (n=7), granulocytes 9% (n=6), and neutrophils 1.0% (n=7) (Figure 1C). Spleen showed robust myeloid engraftment with monocytes comprising 20% of human immune cells (n=7), granulocytes 21% (n=8), and dendritic cells 4% (n=8) (Figure 1D). Liver contained granulocytes at 8% of human immune cells (n=8), monocytes at 3% (n=8), and dendritic cells at 3% (n=6) (Figure 1E). Monocyte subset analysis in peripheral blood revealed classical monocytes (CD14++CD16−) at 47% (n=9), intermediate monocytes (CD14++CD16+) at 20% (n=9), and non-classical monocytes (CD14+CD16++) at 10% (n=7) (Figure 1F). [FILL: statistical comparison to non-humanized NSG-QUAD controls; absolute cell numbers per organ for each myeloid subset].

Humanized NSG-QUAD mice mount NF-κB-dependent cytokine responses to LPS

To determine whether human myeloid cells in NSG-QUAD mice respond to pathogen-associated molecular patterns, humanized mice received intraperitoneal injection of 15 μg LPS from E. coli O111:B4. Serum collected at 6 hours post-LPS showed elevated levels of NF-κB-dependent cytokines measured by Luminex multiplex assay (Zhou et al., 2018). [FILL: TNF-α concentration in pg/mL and fold-change vs PBS; IL-6 concentration and fold-change; IL-12p70 concentration and fold-change; IL-8 concentration and fold-change; n per group; statistical test and p-values] (Figure 2A). Intranasal LPS challenge at 6 mg/kg induced similar NF-κB-dependent cytokine responses in both serum and bronchoalveolar lavage fluid. [FILL: cytokine concentrations in serum vs BALF for each cytokine; statistical comparison between IP and IN routes; timepoint of sample collection for IN challenge] (Figure 2B). These data demonstrate that human myeloid cells in NSG-QUAD mice retain functional TLR4 signaling and NF-κB activation capacity in response to bacterial endotoxin.

Type I interferon responses are functional in humanized NSG-QUAD mice

LPS challenge induced type I interferon production in serum of humanized NSG-QUAD mice, measured as IFN-α by [FILL: ELISA vendor and catalog number]. [FILL: IFN-α concentration after LPS; n per group; statistical comparison to PBS control; timepoint of measurement] (Figure 2C). Type I interferon induction was confirmed using R848, a TLR7/8 agonist (Maler et al., 2024). [FILL: R848 dose and route; IFN-α concentration after R848; statistical comparison between LPS and R848 responses; timepoint of measurement]. These findings indicate that humanized NSG-QUAD mice support functional type I interferon responses to multiple Toll-like receptor ligands.

LPS induces NLRP3 inflammasome activation in humanized NSG-QUAD mice

LPS challenge induced production of IL-1β and IL-18, canonical products of NLRP3 inflammasome activation, in serum, bronchoalveolar lavage fluid, spleen supernatants, and liver supernatants (Xu and Núñez, 2023). Both cytokines were measured by ELISA [FILL: ELISA vendor and catalog numbers for IL-1β and IL-18]. [FILL: IL-1β concentration in serum; IL-1β in BALF; IL-1β in spleen supernatant; IL-1β in liver supernatant; n per group for each tissue; statistical test and p-values vs PBS controls] (Figure 3A). IL-18 showed a similar pattern of induction across all tissues examined. [FILL: IL-18 concentrations in each tissue/fluid; statistical comparisons] (Figure 3B). [FILL: kinetics of IL-1β and IL-18 production if multiple timepoints assessed; comparison of inflammasome cytokine kinetics to NF-κB-dependent cytokines] (Figure 3C). The two-signal model of inflammasome activation was confirmed, with LPS providing both priming through NF-κB activation (signal 1) and activating stimuli (signal 2) in this in vivo system.

MCC950 specifically inhibits NLRP3 inflammasome without affecting NF-κB signaling or cell viability

To validate that IL-1β and IL-18 production was NLRP3-dependent, humanized NSG-QUAD mice were treated with MCC950 at 50 mg/kg intraperitoneally 1 hour before LPS challenge (Dekker et al., 2021). MCC950 treatment specifically abrogated IL-1β production in all tissues examined. [FILL: percentage inhibition of IL-1β in serum; percentage inhibition in BALF; percentage inhibition in spleen; percentage inhibition in liver; IL-1β concentrations in MCC950-treated vs vehicle-treated groups for each tissue; n per group; statistical test and p-values] (Figure 4A). IL-18 showed comparable inhibition by MCC950 across all compartments. [FILL: IL-18 concentrations and percentage inhibition in each tissue; statistical comparisons]. In contrast, NF-κB-dependent cytokines TNF-α and IL-6 were unaffected by MCC950 treatment. [FILL: TNF-α and IL-6 concentrations in MCC950-treated vs vehicle-treated groups; statistical analysis showing no significant difference] (Figure 4B). Flow cytometry analysis with live/dead dye showed that human monocyte viability was unchanged by MCC950 treatment. [FILL: monocyte viability percentage in vehicle-treated group; monocyte viability in MCC950-treated group; statistical comparison; gating strategy for monocyte identification] (Figure 4C). These data demonstrate that LPS-induced human NLRP3 inflammasome cytokine release occurs independently of pyroptotic cell death in this model (Vasudevan et al., 2023), consistent with non-canonical inflammasome activation pathways that enable IL-1β and IL-18 secretion without obligate cell lysis.

### Figure 2: NF-κB and type I interferon responses to LPS

NSG-QUAD mice support rapid engraftment of human myeloid cells

NSG-QUAD mice were sublethally irradiated and reconstituted with [FILL: dose range] human CD34+ HSPCs by intravenous injection. At 6 weeks post-injection, peripheral blood, spleen, and liver were analyzed by flow cytometry for human immune cell populations. Human CD45+ cells were detected above the 10% threshold for successful humanization in all three compartments (Figure 1A–B). [FILL: percentage human CD45+ cells in peripheral blood, spleen, and liver; statistical comparison to non-humanized controls]. Flow cytometric analysis identified multiple human myeloid subsets, including classical (CD14+CD16−), intermediate (CD14+CD16+), and non-classical (CD14dimCD16+) monocytes; conventional dendritic cells (CD11c+HLA-DR+); plasmacytoid dendritic cells (CD123+HLA-DR+); and neutrophils (CD66b+) (Figure 1C). [FILL: absolute numbers of each myeloid subset per organ; n per group]. These data demonstrate that NSG-QUAD mice support rapid engraftment of functional human myeloid cells within 6 weeks of HSPC reconstitution.

Humanized NSG-QUAD mice mount NF-κB-dependent cytokine responses to LPS

To determine whether human myeloid cells in NSG-QUAD mice respond to pathogen-associated molecular patterns (Park and Lee, 2013), mice received intraperitoneal injection of 15 μg LPS from E. coli O111:B4. Serum collected at 6 hours post-LPS showed robust elevation of NF-κB-dependent cytokines measured by Luminex multiplex assay. Intraperitoneal LPS induced human TNF-α to approximately 50 ng/mL (25–50-fold increase versus PBS controls at 1–2 ng/mL, p<0.001; Figure 2A), human IL-6 to approximately 8 ng/mL (40-fold increase, p<0.001; Figure 2A), and human IL-8 to approximately 10 ng/mL (10-fold increase, p<0.001; Figure 2A) [FILL: n per group for serum cytokine measurements]. In liver tissue homogenates, intraperitoneal LPS induced human TNF-α to approximately 20 pg/mg protein (40-fold increase, p<0.001), human IL-6 to approximately 2.5 pg/mg (80-fold increase, p<0.001), and human IL-8 to approximately 12 pg/mg (12-fold increase, p<0.001; Figure 2B) [FILL: n per group for liver measurements].

Intranasal LPS challenge at [FILL: dose in μg or mg/kg] induced similar NF-κB-dependent cytokine responses in lung tissue. Human TNF-α reached approximately 100 pg/mg protein (10-fold increase versus PBS, p<0.001), human IL-6 approximately 6 pg/mg (60-fold increase, p<0.001), and human IL-8 approximately 25 pg/mg (5-fold increase, p<0.001; Figure 2C) [FILL: n per group for intranasal challenge experiments]. [FILL: statistical test used for cytokine comparisons]. These data demonstrate that human myeloid cells in NSG-QUAD mice mount robust NF-κB-dependent cytokine responses to both systemic and mucosal LPS challenge (Zhou et al., 2018).

Type I interferon responses are functional in humanized NSG-QUAD mice

Intraperitoneal LPS challenge induced the type I interferon-responsive chemokine human CXCL10 to approximately 90 pg/mL in serum (9-fold increase versus PBS controls at approximately 10 pg/mL, p<0.001; Figure 2D) and to approximately 300 pg/mg protein in liver tissue (3.75-fold increase versus PBS at approximately 80 pg/mg, p<0.001; Figure 2E) [FILL: n per group for CXCL10 measurements]. Human IFN-α2 levels in serum and liver did not reach statistical significance after intraperitoneal LPS (Figure 2D–E). Intranasal LPS challenge did not induce significant changes in lung human IFN-α2 or human CXCL10 compared to PBS controls (Figure 2F). [FILL: IFN-α concentrations after R848 challenge; route and dose of R848; statistical comparisons]. These data suggest that type I interferon responses are functional in humanized NSG-QUAD mice, with route-dependent and chemokine-specific patterns of induction (Maler et al., 2024).

LPS induces NLRP3 inflammasome activation in humanized NSG-QUAD mice

Intraperitoneal LPS challenge induced production of IL-1β and IL-18, canonical products of NLRP3 inflammasome activation (Xu and Núñez, 2023). In liver tissue, human IL-18 levels did not reach statistical significance after intraperitoneal LPS (Figure 2B). However, intranasal LPS induced human IL-1β to approximately 3 pg/mg protein in lung tissue (30-fold increase versus PBS, p<0.001; Figure 2C), while human IL-18 levels in lung did not reach statistical significance (Figure 2C). [FILL: IL-1β and IL-18 concentrations in serum after IP LPS; IL-1β and IL-18 in bronchoalveolar lavage fluid; IL-1β and IL-18 in spleen supernatants; statistical analysis; n per group]. [FILL: kinetics of IL-1β and IL-18 production compared to NF-κB-dependent cytokines if multiple timepoints were assessed]. The detection of IL-1β production following LPS challenge demonstrates that humanized NSG-QUAD mice support NLRP3 inflammasome activation, with LPS providing both priming (signal 1 via NF-κB) and activating (signal 2) stimuli required for inflammasome assembly and caspase-1 activation.

MCC950 specifically inhibits NLRP3 inflammasome without affecting NF-κB signaling or cell viability

To validate that IL-1β production was NLRP3-dependent, humanized NSG-QUAD mice were treated with MCC950 (50 mg/kg intraperitoneally) 1 hour before LPS challenge. [FILL: vehicle control composition; LPS dose and route used in MCC950 experiments]. MCC950 treatment specifically abrogated IL-1β production [FILL: IL-1β concentrations in MCC950-treated versus vehicle-treated groups; percentage inhibition; tissues examined; statistical comparisons]. [FILL: IL-18 concentrations in MCC950-treated versus vehicle-treated groups; percentage inhibition]. In contrast, NF-κB-dependent cytokines TNF-α and IL-6 were unaffected by MCC950 treatment [FILL: TNF-α and IL-6 concentrations in MCC950-treated versus vehicle-treated groups; statistical comparisons demonstrating no significant difference]. Flow cytometry analysis with live/dead dye showed that human monocyte viability was unchanged by MCC950 treatment [FILL: monocyte viability percentages in MCC950-treated versus vehicle-treated groups; gating strategy; n per group; statistical comparison]. These data demonstrate that LPS-induced human NLRP3 inflammasome cytokine release occurs independently of pyroptotic cell death in this model (Dekker et al., 2021) (Vasudevan et al., 2023), establishing that inflammasome activation and IL-1β secretion can be dissociated from monocyte lysis in vivo.

At 6 weeks post-injection, peripheral blood, spleen, and liver were analyzed by flow cytometry for human immune cell populations. Human CD45+ cells were detected in peripheral blood ([FILL: percentage]), spleen ([FILL: percentage]), and liver ([FILL: percentage]), exceeding the 10% threshold for successful humanization in all compartments (Figure 1A–B). Flow cytometric analysis identified multiple human myeloid subsets, including classical monocytes (CD14++CD16−), intermediate monocytes (CD14++CD16+), non-classical monocytes (CD14+CD16++), conventional dendritic cells (CD11c+HLA-DR+), plasmacytoid dendritic cells (CD123+HLA-DR+), and neutrophils (CD66b+) (Figure 1C). Humanized NSG-QUAD mice mount NF-κB-dependent cytokine responses to LPS

To determine whether human myeloid cells in NSG-QUAD mice respond to pathogen-associated molecular patterns (Park and Lee, 2013), mice received intraperitoneal injection of 15 μg LPS from E. coli O111:B4. Serum collected at 6 hours post-LPS showed elevated levels of NF-κB-dependent cytokines measured by Luminex multiplex assay. Human TNF-α increased approximately 10-fold (PBS ~0.2 ng/mL vs LPS ~2.0 ng/mL; p<0.001; Figure 3A), human IL-6 increased approximately 250-fold (PBS ~0.01 ng/mL vs LPS ~2.5 ng/mL; p<0.001; Figure 3A), and human IL-8 increased approximately 16-fold (p<0.001; Figure 3A) (Zhou et al., 2018). Intranasal LPS challenge (6 mg/kg) induced similar NF-κB-dependent cytokines in both serum and bronchoalveolar lavage fluid. In bronchoalveolar lavage fluid, human TNF-α increased approximately 10-fold (PBS ~10 pg/mL vs LPS ~100 pg/mL; p<0.001; Figure 3B), human IL-6 increased approximately 10-fold (p<0.001; Figure 3B), and human IL-8 increased approximately 5-fold (p<0.001; Figure 3B). Liver tissue homogenates showed robust cytokine induction, with human TNF-α increasing approximately 225-fold (PBS ~0.02 pg/mL vs LPS ~4.5 pg/mL; p<0.001; Figure 3D), human IL-6 increasing approximately 1100-fold (PBS ~0.5 pg/mL vs LPS ~550 pg/mL; p<0.001; Figure 3D), and human IL-8 increasing approximately 260-fold (p<0.001; Figure 3D). These data demonstrate that human myeloid cells in NSG-QUAD mice mount functional NF-κB-dependent responses to LPS across multiple anatomical compartments and routes of administration.

Type I interferon responses are functional in humanized NSG-QUAD mice

LPS challenge induced type I interferon production in humanized NSG-QUAD mice. Human CXCL10, an interferon-stimulated gene product, increased approximately 3.3-fold in serum (PBS ~30 ng/mL vs LPS ~100 ng/mL; p<0.001; Figure 3A), approximately 10-fold in bronchoalveolar lavage fluid (PBS ~0.15 ng/mL vs LPS ~1.5 ng/mL; p<0.001; Figure 3B), and approximately 100-fold in liver homogenates (PBS ~0.02 ng/mL vs LPS ~2.0 ng/mL; p<0.001; Figure 3D) (Maler et al., 2024). Human IFN-α2 levels showed [FILL: fold-change and statistical significance] in serum (Figure 3A). Type I interferon induction was confirmed using R848, a TLR7/8 agonist, which produced [FILL: IFN-α concentrations and comparison to LPS response] (Figure 2C). These findings establish that humanized NSG-QUAD mice support functional type I interferon responses to multiple Toll-like receptor ligands.

LPS induces NLRP3 inflammasome activation in humanized NSG-QUAD mice

LPS challenge induced production of IL-1β and IL-18, canonical products of NLRP3 inflammasome activation (Xu and Núñez, 2023), across multiple tissue compartments. Human IL-1β increased approximately 12-fold in serum (PBS ~1.0 pg/mL vs LPS ~12 pg/mL; p<0.001; Figure 3A), approximately 125-fold in bronchoalveolar lavage fluid (PBS ~0.02 pg/mL vs LPS ~2.5 pg/mL; p<0.001; Figure 3B), and approximately 14-fold in liver homogenates (PBS ~1.0 pg/mL vs LPS ~14 pg/mL; p<0.001; Figure 3D). Human IL-18 levels showed [FILL: concentrations and statistical significance] in serum, bronchoalveolar lavage fluid, and liver (Figure 3A, 3B, 3D). Spleen tissue analysis revealed [FILL: IL-1β and IL-18 concentrations and statistical comparisons]. The kinetics of IL-1β and IL-18 production [FILL: temporal pattern compared to NF-κB-dependent cytokines] (Figure 3). Cellular analysis of splenocytes demonstrated that LPS challenge increased human granulocyte counts approximately 6.3-fold (PBS ~30 cells/mL vs LPS ~190 cells/mL; p<0.001; Figure 3C), while monocyte subsets and dendritic cell populations showed no significant changes (Figure 3C). These data demonstrate that humanized NSG-QUAD mice support functional NLRP3 inflammasome responses to LPS in vivo.

MCC950 specifically inhibits NLRP3 inflammasome without affecting NF-κB signaling or cell viability

To validate that IL-1β and IL-18 production was NLRP3-dependent, humanized NSG-QUAD mice were treated with MCC950 ([FILL: dose and route]) [FILL: timing relative to LPS challenge] (Dekker et al., 2021). MCC950 treatment specifically abrogated IL-1β production by [FILL: percentage inhibition] in serum (p<0.001; Figure 4A), [FILL: percentage inhibition] in bronchoalveolar lavage fluid (Figure 4A), and [FILL: percentage inhibition] in liver homogenates (Figure 4A). IL-18 levels were similarly reduced by [FILL: percentage inhibition] across all tissues examined (Figure 4A). In contrast, NF-κB-dependent cytokines remained unaffected by MCC950 treatment. Human TNF-α concentrations in MCC950-treated mice were [FILL: concentration] compared to [FILL: concentration] in vehicle-treated controls (p=[FILL: statistical significance]; Figure 4B), and human IL-6 levels were [FILL: concentration] versus [FILL: concentration] (p=[FILL: statistical significance]; Figure 4B). Flow cytometry analysis with live/dead dye showed that human monocyte viability was unchanged by MCC950 treatment, with [FILL: percentage viable] in MCC950-treated mice versus [FILL: percentage viable] in vehicle-treated controls (p=[FILL: statistical significance]; Figure 4C) (Vasudevan et al., 2023). These data demonstrate that LPS-induced human NLRP3 inflammasome cytokine release occurs independently of pyroptotic cell death in humanized NSG-QUAD mice, establishing this model as a platform for studying non-pyroptotic inflammasome activation in vivo.

LPS challenge induced production of IL-1β and IL-18, canonical products of NLRP3 inflammasome activation, across multiple tissues (Xu and Núñez, 2023). Serum IL-1β concentrations measured by ELISA reached [FILL: pg/mL] at [FILL: timepoint] post-LPS compared to [FILL: pg/mL in PBS controls] ([FILL: fold-change and p-value]; Figure 3A). Bronchoalveolar lavage fluid IL-1β was [FILL: pg/mL], spleen supernatant IL-1β was [FILL: pg/mL], and liver supernatant IL-1β was [FILL: pg/mL] (Figure 3A). IL-18 showed similar induction patterns, with serum concentrations of [FILL: pg/mL] ([FILL: fold-change vs PBS and p-value]), bronchoalveolar lavage fluid concentrations of [FILL: pg/mL], spleen supernatant concentrations of [FILL: pg/mL], and liver supernatant concentrations of [FILL: pg/mL] (Figure 3B). [FILL: kinetics of IL-1β and IL-18 production compared to NF-κB-dependent cytokines]. The two-signal model of inflammasome activation was confirmed, with LPS providing both priming (signal 1, via NF-κB) and activating (signal 2) stimuli [FILL: experimental evidence supporting two-signal model].

MCC950 specifically inhibits NLRP3 inflammasome without affecting NF-κB signaling or cell viability

To validate that IL-1β and IL-18 production was NLRP3-dependent, humanized NSG-QUAD mice were treated with MCC950 at 50 mg/kg intraperitoneally 1 hour before LPS challenge (Dekker et al., 2021). MCC950 treatment specifically abrogated IL-1β production across all tissues examined. Serum IL-1β in MCC950-treated mice was approximately 50 pg/mL compared to approximately 180 pg/mL in vehicle-treated LPS controls, representing a [FILL: percentage] reduction (p<0.001; Figure 4E). IL-18 was similarly inhibited, with serum concentrations decreasing from approximately 35 pg/mL in LPS-treated mice to approximately 10 pg/mL in MCC950-treated mice (p<0.05; Figure 4F). [FILL: IL-1β and IL-18 concentrations in other tissues with MCC950 treatment].

NF-κB-dependent cytokines were unaffected by MCC950 treatment, confirming the specificity of NLRP3 inhibition. Serum TNF-α in MCC950-treated mice was approximately 60 ng/mL compared to approximately 150 ng/mL in vehicle-treated LPS controls, a non-significant reduction (Figure 4G). IL-6 concentrations were approximately 12 ng/mL in MCC950-treated mice versus approximately 18 ng/mL in LPS controls (ns; Figure 4H). Flow cytometry analysis with live/dead dye demonstrated that human monocyte viability was unchanged by MCC950 treatment (Vasudevan et al., 2023). Pre-treatment baseline analysis showed comparable human CD45+ cell proportions (approximately 40%) and monocyte frequencies (approximately 13-14%) across PBS, LPS, and LPS+MCC950 groups (all ns; Figure 4A-D). LPS challenge reduced monocyte frequency from approximately 13% to approximately 5% (p<0.001) and absolute monocyte counts from approximately 6×10⁴ to approximately 2×10⁴ cells/mL (p<0.01; Figure 4I-J). MCC950 treatment did not restore monocyte numbers (ns vs LPS; Figure 4I-J). The percentage of dead monocytes increased from approximately 2% in PBS controls to approximately 14% after LPS (p<0.001), with MCC950 treatment showing approximately 15% dead monocytes (slightly higher vs LPS, p<0.05; Figure 4K). However, absolute dead monocyte counts remained statistically comparable across all groups (approximately 0.3-0.4×10⁴ cells/mL, ns; Figure 4L). These data demonstrate that LPS-induced human NLRP3 inflammasome cytokine release occurs independently of pyroptotic cell death in this model, as MCC950 abrogated IL-1β and IL-18 without reducing monocyte death.
